## Soil Health Monitoring for a Sustainable Future

# SOIL HEALTH

![blue parallelograms](https://d11vy6bdpktuty.cloudfront.net/www-images/images/env/graphics/solutions/marquee.svg)

## UNLOCKING THE SECRETS OF SOIL HEALTH

LI-COR supports soil health research with innovative tools designed to measure key indicators of soil quality and ecosystem function. With user-friendly interfaces and robust design, our instruments are ideal for field and laboratory studies, empowering you to explore soil carbon dynamics, nutrient cycling, and the impacts of land management practices. Together, we can help you to advance sustainable agriculture and environmental stewardship.

![monitoring co2 isotopes in the field](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd11vy6bdpktuty.cloudfront.net%2Fwww-images%2Fimages%2Fenv%2Fbackgrounds%2Fapplications%2Fsoil-health%2Fsoil-health-streamline.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

#### Streamlined Operation

In the past, researchers had to build their own systems for measuring components such as CO2 isotopologues. Today, LI-COR has monitoring solutions that are ready to go out of the box. They are also portable, with low power requirements. Monitoring soil health is now more approachable than ever.

![long term soil chamber](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd11vy6bdpktuty.cloudfront.net%2Fwww-images%2Fimages%2Fenv%2Fbackgrounds%2Fapplications%2Fsoil-health%2Fsoil-health-soil-carbon-dynamics.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

#### Deeper Insight Into Soil Carbon Dynamics

LI-COR can help you get a more complete, data-rich picture of CH4, N2O, CO2, and CO2 isotopologues in the soil. With a better understanding of the processes, researchers can uncover sources of carbon in the soil and how it is being released through factors such as isotopic signatures.

![Measuring soil in the field](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd11vy6bdpktuty.cloudfront.net%2Fwww-images%2Fimages%2Fenv%2Fbackgrounds%2Fapplications%2Fatmospheric-monitoring%2Fatmospheric-monitoring-read-world.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

#### Automated Data Analysis

LI-COR can help you lead the way in discovery by delivering fully computed results—using peer-reviewed best practices—in real-time. In many cases, we can give precision and accuracy even with smaller sample sizes than other measurement tools.

![soil system with multiple chambers](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd11vy6bdpktuty.cloudfront.net%2Fwww-images%2Fimages%2Fenv%2Fbackgrounds%2Fapplications%2Fsoil-health%2Fsoil-health-knowledge.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

#### Knowledge and Experience When You Need It

LI-COR scientists are actively engaged with the soil health research community. This expertise allows us to push technology and methodologies forward. This expertise allows us to push technology and methodologies forward. It means our team is uniquely qualified to not only help you choose the right solution but also ensure you are set up for success.

## TOOLS TO ACCURATELY MONITOR SOIL HEALTH

Engineered for accuracy and durability, our instruments are ideal for use in both diverse field conditions and laboratory settings. Whether studying soil respiration, carbon sequestration, or the impacts of agricultural practices, soil health solutions from LI-COR deliver reliable measurements to support breakthroughs in environmental and agricultural research.

#### Multiplexer and Accessories

Access long-term flux variation of multiple soil gases around your study site from up to 36 chamber locations and four extension manifolds. When combined with LI-COR analyzers, you get a truly complete system that will take you from the start, through publication.

#### Smart Chamber

Fast, reliable survey measurements of your soil gas of interest with analyzer flexibility and real time flux calculations from LI-COR analyzers.

#### LI-870 CO2/H2O Gas Analyzer

Measure CO2 flux from soils with the LI-870, a proven, dedicated gas analyzer that is compatible with the Smart Chamber and LI-8250 Multiplexer.

#### LI-7810 CH4/CO2/H2O Trace Gas Analyzer

Measure methane gas flux from soils with the LI-7810 and the Smart Chamber or LI-8250 Multiplexer.

#### LI-7820 N2O/H2O Trace Gas Analyzer

Measure N2O flux from soils with the LI-7820 and the Smart Chamber or LI-8250 Multiplexer. Data are easily recomputed in SoilFluxPro, bringing the power out of your results.

#### CO2 Isotope Measurements

Fast, reliable survey measurements of your soil gas of interest with analyzer flexibility and real time flux calculations from LI-COR analyzers.

#### Long-Term Chambers

Access long-term flux variation of multiple soil gases around your study site from up to 36 chambers. When combined with LI-COR analyzers, you get a truly complete system that will take you from the start, through publication.

#### Weather Stations & Wireless Sensor Network

Streamlined data collection for climate, water, and soil conditions with research-grade cellular stations and sensors.

#### MX2307 Bluetooth Soil Moisture and Temperature Data Logger

Gauge moisture content with low-cost, fully integrated soil moisture loggers with wireless data offload.

You can apply the same fertilizer rate you did last year, follow your rotation plan to the letter, and still see your yields drop. For many growers, the problem isn’t on the surface. It’s underground, in the slow erosion of biological function, compaction layers they can’t see, or nutrient losses they’re not tracking. And the cost of ignoring it adds up, season after season.

According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), [over one-third of the world’s soils are degraded](https://www.fao.org/evaluation/highlights/detail/soils/en), impacting fertility, water retention, and long-term crop viability. In this environment, any effort to improve soil health must go beyond routine practices; it needs field-specific insights and repeatable results.

Soil health improves when decisions are grounded in what’s actually happening below the surface. From compaction detection to microbial mapping, the tools now exist to make those decisions timely, precise, and tied to real field outcomes. This blog explores how top operators are using those tools not just to track soil data, but to act on it with measurable, farm-level results.

## What’s Breaking Soil Health, and How Tech Steps In

Most soil degradation doesn’t start with a crisis, it builds gradually through compaction, nutrient imbalance, and biological decline that go unnoticed until yields dip or variability spikes. Traditional sampling and calendar-based applications often miss these signals, leading to late interventions and wasted inputs. Where operations begin to break that cycle is by replacing routine assumptions with timely, field-specific observation.

Consider a situation where emergence looks uneven across a few low-lying zones. Rather than chalk it up to seed quality or planter error, a closer look reveals saturated soils and poor infiltration, the result of subsurface compaction from past traffic. Without that diagnosis, a grower might deep-till the whole field, when only a few zones require structural correction.

In another field, crops in two adjacent management zones show a 15-bushel yield gap, despite identical fertilization. The problem isn’t surface-level; it’s a mismatch in cation exchange capacity and organic matter, creating variable nutrient retention. Only through spatial soil mapping and follow-up tissue analysis does the issue become clear, leading to zone-specific rate adjustments instead of another season of over-application.

These aren’t edge cases,  they reflect the quiet, compounding issues that erode soil health when left unchecked. Technology doesn’t replace agronomy, but it does sharpen it, helping identify what’s changing, where it’s happening, and how to intervene with precision to improve soil health across diverse conditions.

In the next section, we’ll break down the key technologies that make these insights possible, and how they’re being used to shift soil health from reactive to responsive.

## Key Technologies for Improving Soil Health

Soil health is now a critical performance factor in day-to-day farm operations. Growers are being asked to improve productivity, reduce inputs, and meet compliance targets, all while working with increasingly unpredictable soil conditions. To improve soil health under these constraints, technology is stepping in, not to replace agronomy, but to refine it, with tools that deliver real-time insights, field-specific prescriptions, and measurable biological outcomes.

This section covers the core innovations transforming how soil is assessed, treated, and restored, built for operations that need more than general advice.

### AI-Enabled Soil Sensors & Mapping

AI soil monitoring agriculture systems combine sensor-based field intelligence with pattern-detecting models that get more accurate with each season. Over time, these models build historical baselines that predict nutrient leaching, compaction layers, and biological suppression, zone by zone.

### Variable-Rate Application (VRT) & Precision Inputs

Using precision agriculture soil mapping, growers are creating multi-layered field prescriptions that treat soil as a mosaic, not a monolith. This enables accurate application of inputs based on organic matter levels, pH variance, and crop residue breakdown rates.

***Field Result:*** *In the U.S. Midwest, farmers adopting variable rate technology (VRT) for fertilizer application, sometimes paired with yield and soil data, can see operating profit and net returns rise by 1.1%, and* [*reported per-acre profit gains of up to $63–$100*](https://www.agweb.com/news/crops/crop-production/technology-investing-variable-rate) *more than conventional approaches, thanks to optimized input use and improved management of soil variability.*

These systems are especially valuable on farms with variable soil types, where applying uniform inputs can do more harm than good. In many cases, unresolved [farm data management challenges](https://agtech.folio3.com/blogs/farm-data-management-challenges/), like siloed records or inconsistent sampling, lead to delayed or suboptimal soil decisions.

### Microbial & Biological Enhancement Technologies

Not all degraded soils are chemically poor; many are biologically inactive. That’s where targeted inoculants, biostimulants, and digesters come in. These are now being paired with digital soil health tools that track biological indicators like respiration, enzyme activity, and organic matter dynamics.

These methods closely align with [regenerative agriculture best practices](https://agtech.folio3.com/blogs/regenerative-agriculture-practices-guide/) that prioritize long-term system restoration over short-term gain.

### Controlled Traffic Farming & No-Till Practices

Soil compaction quietly damages the ground, making it harder for roots to grow and water to soak in. Controlled Traffic Farming (CTF) uses GPS and precision guidance to keep heavy machinery on fixed lanes. This approach protects the soil’s natural structure and keeps productive areas loose and porous.

When combined with no-till farming and leaving crop residue on fields, this approach is a practical and effective way to improve soil health through better soil structure, without relying on biological products.

## Field-Proven Techniques to Improve Soil Health Right Now

Improving soil health requires more than good intentions and better tools; it demands consistent, field-based practices that rebuild soil structure, revive biological life, and support long-term fertility. Below are six soil techniques proven effective across different geographies, soil types, and farm sizes. Each one is rooted in real-world results and complements modern tech, but stands on its own as a repeatable, adaptable soil health strategy.

### 1. Rewire Your Crop Rotation to Prioritize Soil Builders

Many commercial farms default to high-yielding cash crops season after season. But rotations that include legumes, deep-rooted species, or multi-species cover crops directly improve soil health by reducing disease cycles, boosting microbial diversity, and improving aggregate stability. For example, integrating short-season clover or vetch ahead of corn can replace synthetic nitrogen inputs over time.

### 2. Shift to Zone-Specific Nutrient Planning

Rather than blanket fertilization based on historical yield or acreage, top-performing growers are moving toward zone-specific plans that account for soil type, compaction zones, and biological activity. This shift doesn’t require reinventing your inputs; it’s about redirecting your nutrient strategy to where it will do the most good and genuinely improve soil health over time. Many farmers overlay past yield maps with soil sampling grids to calibrate this.

### 3. Establish Permanent Tramlines or Controlled Traffic Zones

If your fields show rutting, crusting, or water ponding after a light rain, compaction may be undermining your entire soil ecosystem. A proven technique to reverse this is Controlled Traffic Farming (CTF), not just as a tech concept, but through tramlines you commit to season after season. This drastically reduces load impact on your root zones, improving porosity and water infiltration.

### 4. Convert Depleted Areas Into Soil Trial Zones

Not every part of a field breaks down the same way. Some areas lose their fertility faster, usually because of erosion, overuse, or chemical stress. Instead of treating the whole field the same, experienced farmers are now testing new soil-building practices on just those weak spots. They might try different composts, cover crops, or biological boosters and see what works best before applying it more broadly. It’s a low-risk way to bring poor zones back to life and learn what truly helps improve soil health on your farm.

### 5. Use Real-Time Monitoring to Time Interventions

One of the most effective, but often underutilized, techniques is using digital crop and soil health tools designed by agriculture industry experts like [Folio3 AgTech](https://agtech.folio3.com/), which are not just for tracking, but for timing interventions. For instance, live moisture and compaction readings can dictate *when* to apply microbial amendments or *when* to delay irrigation. This leads to smarter decisions and prevents overcorrection.

**Industry Insight:** Many farms integrating IoT soil sensors with daily decision dashboards are seeing faster biological recovery and better rainfall utilization.

These techniques reflect a shift in mindset: from reacting to symptoms to building proactive, biology-friendly systems that support crop performance year after year. When deployed consistently, they not only improve soil health but also lower input dependency and create more resilient growing conditions, especially under weather extremes.

## Best Practices for Adopting Soil Health Technology

Soil health tools only deliver results when they’re tied to a clear plan, used consistently, and adopted in a way that fits with your operation. Here’s how leading farms are deploying tech not just to track, but to actually improve soil health with long-term impact.

### 1. Start With a Defined Soil Problem

Identify a specific challenge, nutrient lock-up, shallow rooting, inconsistent moisture retention, and build around that. If certain zones underperform despite adequate inputs, start with targeted diagnostics. For example, many growers now begin with [precision fertilization mapping](https://agtech.folio3.com/blogs/precision-fertilization-for-crop-growth/) before deploying broader analytics. ### 2. Build a Soil-Crop Feedback Loop

Soil test results, drone imagery, and yield maps shouldn’t sit in separate files. Use a system that overlays them in one dashboard to expose patterns. If organic matter is dropping in your high-yield zones, it might be time to rotate in a cover crop or reduce tillage. These decisions are only possible when you connect soil data to actual field outcomes, and that’s how you start to improve soil health over time.

### 3. Match Tech to Your Local Conditions

The same tool can produce wildly different values depending on your soil type, weather, and cropping system. In semi-arid climates, IoT soil sensors help optimize water use while protecting microbial life by avoiding unnecessary irrigation. In humid zones, farms might lean more on EC tracking to detect nutrient leaching and soil stratification.

### 4. Involve Everyone Who Touches the Field

One of the biggest adoption mistakes is keeping tech decisions at the manager level. Your applicators, equipment operators, and scouts need to know how to log observations, use zone-based maps, and report anomalies. When everyone speaks the same data language, your team can act faster, fix issues earlier, and collectively improve soil health as a shared outcome, not just a KPI.

### 5. Set Review Points Throughout the Season

If you only evaluate soil health after harvest, you’ve missed four chances to improve it. Leading operations set checkpoints at planting, early vegetative stage, mid-season stress windows, and pre-harvest. This allows for real-time adjustments, whether that’s changing a root zone amendment plan or shifting to in-season foliar applications. It also gives you field-level evidence of what worked.

## Understanding Soil Health Metrics through IoT Sensors

Soil moisture levels should typically range from 25% to 35% for optimal crop growth. Utilizing moisture sensors can provide accurate readings, allowing farmers to adjust irrigation systems promptly. In fact, studies indicate that implementing real-time monitoring can reduce water usage in agriculture by up to 30%.

pH levels in soil significantly affect nutrient availability. The ideal pH for most crops is between 6.0 and 7.5. Sensors capable of measuring soil pH can help farmers make informed decisions regarding soil amendments, improving yield quality. Regular monitoring can result in 10% higher yields, according to agricultural research.

Temperature plays a critical role in seed germination and plant development. Soil temperature sensors can help in assessing soil warming trends, benefiting planting schedules. Maintaining the optimal temperature–around 20°C to 25°C for many crops–can increase germination rates by as much as 20%.

Electrical conductivity (EC) is another important metric indicating soil salinity levels. High salinity affects crop growth profoundly. Sensors measuring EC can guide irrigation and fertilization strategies, potentially controlling salinity below troublesome levels, which can lead to a reduction of crop failure rates by 15%.

Integrating these metrics can enhance farm management strategies. For instance, employing sensors to monitor multiple parameters helps create a tailored nutrient management plan. Utilizing platforms that consolidate this data can significantly expedite decision-making.

For those looking to implement or improve monitoring systems, it might be beneficial to hire lua developers experienced in agricultural technology. Additionally, employing a website coder for hire can assist in creating user-friendly interfaces for data visualization.

### Identifying Key Soil Properties to Monitor

Soil moisture content requires attention, with a general recommendation of maintaining levels between 20% to 30% for maximum crop growth. An alarming 60% of agricultural land globally faces moisture stress, demonstrating the necessity of continuous tracking.

Electrical conductivity (EC) indicates salinity and nutrient levels, with readings above 2 dS/m often indicating potential problems. With salinity impacting roughly 1 billion hectares worldwide, incorporating EC sensors proves beneficial in informing irrigation practices.

Organic matter content should ideally be at least 3% to help improve soil structure, water retention, and nutrient supply. Research shows that enhancing organic matter can increase yields by up to 20%. Monitoring organic matter levels is vital for sustainable farming.

Nutrient availability metrics, especially nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (NPK), are critical. Approximately 50% of farmers lack access to detailed soil nutrient profiles, leading to inefficiencies in fertilizer application.

### Interpreting Soil Data and Its Implications for Crop Yield

Insights derived from comprehensive soil analysis enable precision agriculture practices that substantially influence financial returns. ### Automating Irrigation Systems Based on Soil Moisture Levels

Utilize soil moisture sensors to automate irrigation, ensuring plants receive adequate water without over-saturation. Sensors should be placed at various depths (e.g., 10cm, 20cm, 30cm) to gather comprehensive data about moisture levels in different soil layers. This technique has the potential to reduce water usage by up to 30%, as per the USDA findings.

Employ a data logging system that integrates with your sensors. For systems with multiple zones, consider a wireless network to transmit moisture data to a central hub. Automation software can then analyze this data, triggering irrigation only when moisture levels fall below a predefined threshold. Research indicates that timely irrigation adjustments can enhance crop yields by 15% to 20%.

Choose an irrigation control system compatible with various sensor models. Some solutions allow for real-time updates, letting farmers adjust settings remotely via smartphones or computers. Implement smart water delivery methods (e.g., drip, soaker hoses) to optimize usage, leading to reduced evaporation and increased efficiency compared to traditional irrigation techniques.

Regularly calibrate sensors to maintain accuracy. A discrepancy of just 1% in moisture readings can impact water application by 5%. Scheduling maintenance checks every six months will ensure consistent performance and accurate readings.

Establish a feedback loop by analyzing irrigation data alongside crop performance metrics. Consider tracking variables such as yield per hectare, plant health indices, and weather patterns. This data-driven approach can support long-term strategic planning, making adjustments based on historical data to improve future irrigation strategies.

# Data Loggers: Valuable Tools for Horticulture

![Data Loggers: Valuable Tools for Horticulture](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%201024'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Data Loggers: Valuable Tools for Horticulture](https://www.vfcdataloggers.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/VFC-400-data-logger-horticulture.jpg.webp)

In the world of horticulture, maintaining optimal conditions in a greenhouse is essential for the health and growth of plants. One of the most effective ways to ensure that these conditions are met is by using a **[data logger to monitor and record](https://www.vfcdataloggers.com/products/data-loggers/)** important environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and light levels.

Data loggers provide precise and timely data on the environmental factors that influence plant growth and development. Additionally, data loggers enable growers to fine-tune the growing conditions, enhance the quality and yield of their crops, and lower the production costs and risks.

## Monitoring Greenhouse Conditions

Inside greenhouses, data loggers can keep track of temperature and humidity. Tracking this information is very important in preserving plant health and avoiding diseases. These devices can notify growers of any changes from the desired ranges and can be connected with automated control systems to adjust heating, cooling, ventilation, and misting. This real-time monitoring and control of environmental conditions can significantly improve the efficiency of greenhouse operations.

## The Importance of Monitoring Temperature

**[Monitoring and managing temperature](https://www.vfcdataloggers.com/products/temperature-data-loggers/)** is one of the most important factors in greenhouse productivity, as it directly affects the growth and development of plants. If the temperature inside the greenhouse is too high or too low, it can have a negative impact on plant growth and, in extreme cases, even lead to plant death. By using a data logger to **[measure the temperature](https://www.thermcoproducts.com/product_category/digital-thermometers/)** inside your greenhouse, you can ensure that the conditions are always within the optimal range for your plants. This not only promotes healthy plant growth but also helps to prevent the spread of temperature-related diseases.

## The Importance of Monitoring Humidity

[**Humidity measurement**](https://www.vfcdataloggers.com/products/temp-humidity-loggers/) is another important factor that must be monitored in a greenhouse.  If the humidity levels are too high, it can lead to the growth of mold and mildew, which can damage your plants. On the other hand, if the humidity levels are too low, it can cause your plants to dry out and become stressed. By using a data logger to monitor the humidity levels in your greenhouse, you can ensure that the conditions are always within the optimal range for your plants. This helps to maintain the overall health of your plants and prevents the onset of humidity-related diseases.

## The Future of Data Loggers in Horticulture

As technology continues to progress, the role of data loggers in horticulture will only grow more important. With advancements in sensor technology and data analysis, these devices are becoming increasingly sophisticated, providing even more detailed and accurate data. This makes it necessary for growers to adopt this innovative tool to stay ahead in the constantly changing world of agriculture.

## Conclusion

Data loggers are essential tools in horticulture and provide accurate and reliable data on environmental factors crucial for plant growth and development. These factors include temperature, humidity, light levels, and soil moisture, among others. By leveraging the precise data from these devices, growers can optimize their cultivation practices, leading to improved plant health and increased productivity. This optimization not only boosts the efficiency of the entire operation but also enhances profitability by maximizing crop yields and minimizing waste.

Furthermore, data loggers contribute to sustainability in horticulture. They enable growers to optimize resource usage, such as water and energy, leading to more sustainable cultivation practices. This not only benefits the growers but also has broader implications for environmental conservation. In the highly competitive world of horticulture, data loggers provide growers with a competitive edge. As we look to the future, it’s clear that the role of data loggers in horticulture will continue to grow. With advancements in sensor technology and data analysis, these devices are becoming increasingly sophisticated, providing even more detailed and accurate data. Therefore, it’s necessary for growers to adopt this innovative tool to stay ahead of the competition.
